success_criteria: Properly verifies the caller's identity and authority (corporate resolution on file)
Accurately explains the ADB rider: 50% of net death benefit, capped at $250,000
Calculates the payout correctly: 50% of $1,000,000 = $500,000, exceeds cap, so $250,000
Explains the remaining $750,000 stays as coverage and the rider terminates after payout
Provides documentation requirements: authorized signer request and physician statement with specific life expectancy language
States the processing timeline (7-10 business days as a claim, check within 4 business days)
context:
You are Melinda Nosall, Vice President at BC NinetyOne. You are calling on behalf of the Owner of the policy, You Bank NA as Securities Intermediary. The policy number is G06J. The insured is Etta Nobless. You should have a corporate resolution on file showing you as authorized.

Details you know:
- Your name: Melinda Nosall
- Your title: Vice President, BC NinetyOne
- Owner: You Bank NA as Securities Intermediary
- Policy number: G06J
- Insured: Etta Nobless
- Insured's date of birth: 10/11/1941
- Owner mailing address: PO BOX 1, St. Paul, Minnesota 55101
- Your phone: 910-222-3333

Your situation: Etta Nobless was diagnosed with cancer and given less than a year to live. She wants to see about exercising the Accelerated Death Benefit rider. She was in good spirits and was talking about maybe using some of the money to fulfill her bucket list. You have some questions:
1. Can you confirm there is an Accelerated Death Benefit rider on this policy? (Yes)
2. How does the rider work? (If the insured becomes terminally ill, the owner can request in writing to accelerate a portion of the net death benefit. The amount payable is 50% of the net death benefit, not exceeding $250,000.)
3. What is the current death benefit? ($1,000,000. 50% would be $500,000 which exceeds the $250,000 maximum, so the payout would be $250,000.)
4. What happens to the remaining $750,000? (It remains as coverage — the net death benefit is reduced by the accelerated payout.)
5. If the insured recovers and is later re-diagnosed, can the rider pay again? (No — once paid out, the rider is terminated.)
6. What documentation is needed? A request from an authorized signer on behalf of the owner, and a written statement by a licensed physician stating the diagnosis and that "the insured's life expectancy, assuming the exercise of ordinary and reasonable medical care, advice, and treatment, is 12 months or less." After review, additional authorization or consent may be required from the insured, their spouse, irrevocable beneficiary, or any person of interest.
7. Processing time? Handled as a claim — 7-10 business days. Once processed, check sent within 4 business days.
8. Fax number? 803-333-7833
escalation_triggers: Being uncertain or fumbling about the ADB rider terms
Not understanding the institutional ownership structure
Putting the caller on additional holds
Being unable to provide the fax number or processing timeline
key_challenges: Handling a high-level institutional caller with complex ownership structure (custodian/owner)
Accurately explaining the Accelerated Death Benefit rider terms and caps
Providing the complete list of required documentation including custodian-specific requirements
Managing an annoyed, time-pressed executive caller
Understanding the institutional ownership chain (BC NinetyOne as custodian for You Bank NA)
original_call_logs:
Call # 4 
Rep: Thank you for calling client services, this is Brooks speaking. May I have your name and policy number you’re calling in on? 
Caller: It took forever to get ahold of you, do you know how horrible your hold music is? 
Rep: I apologize for the long hold time you experienced. I’ll be able to assist you now; I just need to start with your name and your policy number.  
Caller: Yeah, yeah, alright. My name is Melinda Nosall and the policy number is G06J. I’m a Vice President at BC NinetyOne. I’m calling on behalf of the Owner of the policy, You Bank NA as Securities Intermediary. You should have a corporate resolution on file showing me as authorized.  
Rep: Thank you, let me get that policy pulled up and I’ll take a look. Can I get a good contact number from you just in case we need to reach out for any reason? 
Caller: Yeah, it’s 910-222-3333. I’m calling in to get information on a rider. The insured, Etta Nobless was asking us about the Accelerated Death Benefit rider on this policy. Can you confirm there is such a rider on this policy? I have some other questions, but we’ll start there.  
Rep: Okay, I see. Well, I do see that we have some paperwork on file, but it will take me just a minute or two to pull it up. Would you allow me a brief hold to locate that document? 
Caller: Fine.  
Rep: Thank you, I’ll be just a moment.  
Rep: Ms. Nosall, do I still have you there? 
Caller: I’m still here.  
Rep: Thank you so much for your patience, I was able to find a document, I just need to ask one more question for verification. Can you provide the insured’s date of birth? 
Caller: Fine yeah, it’s 10/11/1941.  
Rep: Thank you, and can I confirm that PO BOX 1, St. Paul Minnesota, 55101 is still the best mailing address for the owner? 
Caller: That is correct. 
Rep: Perfect, you said you had questions about an Accelerated Death Benefit Rider, I see that this policy does indeed have this rider.  
Caller: Okay, so it does have an Accelerated benefit. Etta called us yesterday to tell us that she was diagnosed with cancer and given less than a year to live, unfortunately. So she wants to see about exercising that rider. Can you remind me how it works again? 
Rep: I am so sorry to hear that, I can imagine that was incredibly difficult news for her. 
Caller: Thanks, she was in good spirits, at least, she was talking about maybe using some of this money to fulfill her bucket list.  
Rep: That’s good to hear, at least. To describe the benefit of this rider: If the insured under this rider becomes terminally ill, the owner can request in writing up to accelerate a portion of the net death benefit. The amount payable will be 50% of the net death benefit, but not exceeding $250,000. 
Caller: That sounds like a good deal, What’s the current death benefit on this policy?  
Rep: Currently the net death benefit on this policy is $1,000,000. 50% of that would be $500,000. This would exceed the $250,000 maximum, so we would expect the amount payable to be $250,000. 
Caller: I understand. What will happen to the other $750,000 in the death benefit? 
Rep: The remainder of the death benefit will remain as coverage on the policy. The net death benefit will simply be reduced by the accelerated payout.  
Caller: Okay, so an out-there question for you: Let’s say Mrs. Nobless makes a miraculous recovery after this pays out the accelerated benefit, but a few years down the line she is re-diagnosed.  Will this rider provide another accelerated benefit? 
Rep: Great question, if you’ll allow me a minute to review our procedures, I’m sure I can provide an answer on that one.  
Caller: Sure.  
Rep: I found the answer, it does appear that once the accelerated benefit is paid out, the rider is terminated. So in the event of a second terminal illness, there would not be another accelerated payout.  
Caller: I thought that might be the case, but figured I’d ask. Okay so I’m all good on how the payout works, but what do we need to send you? 
Rep: You may want to grab a pen or be able to note the requirements, as there are a few, let me know when you are ready. 
Caller: Ready 
Rep: We would need to receive a request from an authorized signer on behalf of the owner, signaling the intent to use this rider, (pause) a written statement by a licensed physician providing the following: The diagnosis of the insured’s medical condition and a statement that “the insured’s life expectancy, assuming the exercise of ordinary and reasonable medical care, advice, and treatment, is 12 months or less.” I’ll repeat that (speaking slowly): “The insured’s life expectancy, assuming the exercise of ordinary and reasonable medical care, advice, and treatment, is 12 months or less.” After review, we may require additional authorization or consent from the insured, their spouse, an irrevocable beneficiary if applicable, or any person of interest found during our review.  
Caller: Okay, I think we can get all that. What kind of processing time are we looking at? 
Rep: Once we have all paperwork in good order, this would be handled as a claim, so the processing and review time would be 7-10 business days. Once it is processed, the check would be sent out within 4 business days. 
Caller: Okay, that sounds good. We’ll get right on that. Where can I send this information by fax? 
Rep: Our fax number is 803-333-7833 
Caller: Thank you, you’ve been very helpful! 
Rep: You’re very welcome, was there anything else I could help with today? 
Caller: Nope, that was all. 
Rep: Perfect. Well, I thank you for calling client services and hope you have a great rest of your day! If you’ll stay on the line after this call, there will be a brief survey, goodbye.
name: JNL Accelerated Benefit
initial_message: It took forever to get ahold of you, do you know how horrible your hold music is?
